messages.content: You are an expert EU proposal writer for IRIS Technology Solutions, a photonics and NIR spectroscopy company. Write in first-person plural (we/our). Use precise technical language. Follow EU Horizon proposal conventions: numbered tasks (**Task X.Y**), clear objectives, evidence-based claims. Focus on specific, measurable objectives linked to the call expected outcomes. Explain why the project is ambitious and beyond the current state of the art.
Write the Objectives and ambition section for a Horizon Europe proposal.

1.1 Objectives  
Due to our unique properties, lightweight and versatility, plastics have outgrown most man-made materials. We are especially used throughout the agri-food value chain (the waste thereof will be referred to in our proposal as AWP: agri-food waste plastics1). From the globally produced 350 Mtons (million metric tons) of plastics per year, our target sectors consume in the range of 50% (packaging 40%, agriculture 3.5%)2 and, in fact, they represent even a greater share of single and short-term use plastics. Nevertheless, the challenges linked to their end-of-life have long been under environmental scrutiny. Indeed, only 31% of plastic are currently recycled and an excessive 30-40% are landfilled, this being due to infra-developed technologies or to their unsatisfactory economic viability 3. In addition, according to the Ellen MacArthur Foundation 4, as much as 32% end up out of controlled waste management systems and leak in our environment.  
Plastic durability is a further challenge for agricultural films, which collection for materials recycling at end of life is broadly jeopardized due to their fouling. The affordable biodegradable plastic alternatives cannot degrade in the soil but could be sent to composting plants adding a significant economic hurdle to their greater material cost and lower performance than mostly used fossil counterparts. As of 2017, approximately 6,300 Mtons of agricultural films plastic waste had been generated, around 9% of which had been recycled, 12% was incinerated, and 79% was accumulated in agri-fields (buried), landfills or the natural environment.  
Likewise, more than half of the used food packaging collected in municipal wastes cannot be easily recycled due to the presence of leftover products and the fact that- as for certain mulching films and for most of food packaging, they contain complex laminated materials preventing their reprocessing. As such, most of the flexible and/or multilayer packaging are currently incinerated or even landfilled. This amounts for in the range of 13 Mtons per year in Europe5.  
Our project, RECOVER, is a disruptive RIA proposal built upon the actual needs to solve the contamination of agro-fields with non-biodegradable agro-plastics from our end users as well as to improve municipal waste handling by decreasing drastically the packaging fraction going to unsustainable waste management routes.  
We intend to reach our ambitious objectives applying biotech solutions that will include symbiotic bioprocesses between previously reported high powered microorganisms through microbiotic systems vectored by several insect species. Indeed, one of the main innovations in our proposal is the combination of endogenous and exogenous microorganisms and their inoculation on insects. This will allow us to achieve higher levels of biodegradation and direct conversion (at the same time) of AWP in insect by-products such as chitin-chitosan (high value as bioplastics raw materials and other interesting industrial applications).  
A great attention will be dedicated to establishing simple and reproducible environmental conditions that can be replicated in the farms or municipalities as a profitable activity that can benefit the whole value chains from plastic producers to the end-users involved in our project. As such, the business model, Life Cycle Assessment (LCA), Life Cycle Cost (LCC) and Technology Advanced Analysis (TEA) will be studied throughout our project. To validate the new metabolic routes and biotech tools, further certifications will be carried out regarding the actual biodegradation reached and innovative tools will be developed to support the implementation of the new process on suitable pre-sorted plastics fractions.  
It is estimated that tapping upon a share of the yearly used agricultural films and non-recyclable food packaging (43% of plastics consumed in EU), the newly developed organic recycling route will help increase plastic recycling in EU by 12% (details in section 2) generating ~3.460 new jobs and an annual turnover of €724 million by 2030 (details specified in section 2). Furthermore, it will reduce CO2 emissions derived from the AWP previously incinerated. The proposed bioremediation approach could be further expanded to recover plastic in existing landfills and highly polluted areas.  
All in all, whereas there are huge challenges with the currently available end of life treatment of the plastics used in the agriculture and food industries, our project, RECOVER, will help provide novel biotechnological solutions applying microorganisms, novel enzymes, earthworms and insects to degrade conventional plastic packaging and agricultural films waste streams but also result in new feedstocks for the bio-based industries. The process will be optimized and performed either ex-situ in insect rearing chamber and composting reactors (preferred route for non-recyclable plastics from municipal solid waste or for agricultural films that can be collected easily) or in-situ in the case of bioremediating soil pollution, by e.g. mulching films, further contributing to the long-term removal of non-biodegradable polymers from the environment. In the former case, our project will also deliver chitin/chitosan extracted from the vectorized insects after their use which can in turn be a new source of bioplastics used in our target sectors for e.g. improved mulching films and active packaging, as well as in new fertilizers valorizing in cascade any by-products of the process. In addition, RECOVER can solve the microplastics pollution at the level of both industrial composting and soil.  
To achieve our goals, we have defined a 48-month work plan with the following specific objectives, linked to their specific WPs and performance indicators (used as milestones for project implementation):  
Objective 1. To quantify and characterize AWP in order to select the proper ones for our project and define feasible logistics for collection, sorting and pre-treatment (Linked to WP1)  
We will make a comprehensive inventory and report on plastic types, collection strategies, cost, quality, safety and sustainability of AWP collection and possible pre-treatment. All partners will be involved in this task and the collected data constitutes key elements to set the further approaches to treat AWP and our RECOVER business strategies. KPI1: by month 6 (MS1), a selection of 4-6 types of most representative fossil plastics (including in each case virgin plastics and AWP counterparts) picked individually from MSW or agricultural films will be available in order to provide normalized samples to all the WPs. By month 12 (MS2), we will obtain the matrix for the definition of the classification, characterization and recommended biodegradation procedure. By month 12 (MS3), we will define the abiotic pre-treatment to be used depending on the mixture of AWP to biodegrade, and of micro-macro organism suitable for AWP biodegradation.  
Objective 2. To select microbial communities, novel synthetic enzymes, earthworms and insects for AWP biodegradation (Linked to WP2)  
We will identify strains with greater capacity to use AWP as carbon source and explore the coding space of enzyme families in a focused fashion to combine independently evolved enzyme optimizations from different lineages of the same species, and from independent lineages that would have no capabilities to recombine and co-evolve enzymes in Nature. At least 4-6 species of insect and 2 species of earthworms for biodegradation of AWP will be tested. KPI2: by month 12 (MS4), we will select 3-4 microbial consortia and 2-3 enzymes. By month 18 (MS5), we will have 2-3 microbial/enzyme pools for each earthworm and insect selected. By month 24 (MS6), novel enzymes and new strategy for directed evolution will be available.  
Objective 3. To upscale the production of the suitable biotechnological solutions and combine them (Linked to WP3)  
We will produce stable plastic-degrading microbial/enzyme consortia (PMC) and worms and insects/larvae fortified with PMC for enhanced AWP degradation capability. KPI3: by month 24 at least 2-4 stable microbial/enzyme inoculums (MS7) and 1-2 fortified insects and earthworms (MS8) will be available for the upscaling AWP biodegradation trials in WP4.  
Objective 4. To upscale and monitor biodegradation capacities of the fortified microbial consortia (FMC) (Linked to WP4)  
We will test the inoculum from the previous WP by adding it in soil as bioremediation to AWP pollution as well as to feed earthworms/insects larvae permitting them to improve AWP biodegradation in bioreactors. Monitoring and modelling strategies will be put in place to optimize the process. KPI4: by month 30 (MS9), we will define process conditions for enhanced AWP biodegradation (MS9). By month 36, we will have upscaled ex-situ and in situ AWP biodegradation processes performed (MS10) and biodegradation modelling established (MS11).  
Objective 5. To develop added value downstream products (Linked to WP5)  
We will set up a green process to extract chitosan from the waste insects involved in the AWP degradation. The valorization of chitin and derived chitosan will be tested as additive in active plastic for packaging and mulching films providing anti-microbial and self-healing properties. Biofertilizer from vermicompost, insect manure and residues from chitosan extraction will be tested. KPI5: by month 36 (MS12) we will have 2-3 fertilizer products based on vermicompost, insect and/or chitin available. By month 42 (MS13), we will define the extraction process for chitin from our RECOVER by-products. By month 48 (MS14), we will have
